records.scientific_name: Galerina marginata; older names/synonyms in field guides may include Galerina autumnalis, Galerina oregonensis, Galerina unicolor, and Galerina venenata.
records.poisoning_effects: Delayed gastrointestinal illness, apparent improvement, liver failure, kidney failure, and possible death.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Rusty-brown spores on a small wood-growing mushroom mean stop.
records.field_identification: Small to medium brown/tawny mushroom growing on rotting wood, often in clusters. Cap is moist/sticky when fresh, honey yellow to cinnamon-brown, often fading as it dries and becoming two-toned. Rusty brown spore print is a critical separator from white-spored edible wood mushrooms. Thin ring or ring zone may be present but can disappear with age. Stem darkens brown to reddish brown from the base upward. Can fruit in spring and fall, and may appear outside those windows.
records.overview: Searchable danger entry for Deadly Galerina. Included because it can be confused with edible mushrooms in Upper Michigan / northern Great Lakes foraging contexts.
records.look_alike_notes: Velvet Foot: Velvet Foot has a white spore print and a dark velvety stem base; Deadly Galerina has rusty-brown spores and may have a fragile ring/ring zone. Honey Mushroom Complex: Honey mushrooms have white spore prints. Galerina has rusty-brown spores and can grow from the same wood, sometimes close enough to make mixed collections dangerous. Pholiota Group: Pholiota often has darker brown spores and scaly/sticky caps, but clustered wood growth is similar enough that it should not be treated as edible without strong ID.

substrate: rotting hardwood and conifer wood; stumps, logs, buried wood
ring: thin ring/ring zone often present when young; may disappear
stem_color: dark brown to reddish brown toward base
stem_feature: thin ring or ring zone may be present
mushroom_profile.gill_attachment: attached to slightly descending; check source photos/keys for specimen-level confirmation

records.mushroom_card_note: Do not eat small brown mushrooms from wood unless the identification is expert-level and the spore print rules out Galerina. For this guide: rusty-brown spores on a small wood mushroom means stop.
records.toxicity_notes: Contains amatoxins; delayed symptoms may progress to liver and kidney failure.
records.risk_reason: Contains amatoxins and can be confused with edible wood-growing mushrooms such as honey mushrooms and wild enoki.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Liver and kidneys; delayed gastrointestinal illness may be followed by apparent improvement, then liver failure, kidney failure, and possible death.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Small brown mushrooms on dead wood overlap with edible wood mushrooms. Rusty-brown spore print is the key separator from honey mushrooms and wild enoki, which have white spore prints. A ring may be present but subtle or damaged.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat small brown mushrooms from wood unless the species is confirmed and the spore print matches the edible target. For honey mushrooms and wild enoki, a rusty-brown spore print means do not eat.
records.caution_reason: Amatoxin risk; small brown wood mushrooms can be mixed with edible wood species unless every specimen and spore print is checked.
records.danger_notes: Delayed symptoms are more dangerous than quick stomach upset. If vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal pain begins 6 or more hours after eating wild mushrooms, treat it as a medical emergency and contact Poison Control or emergency care.

records.scientific_name: Gyromitra species likely to be encountered around Michigan morel season, especially Gyromitra esculenta ('beefsteak morel') and related false morel groups. This entry is for Gyromitra, not all Verpa species, though Verpa should remain linked as non-edible morel look-alikes.
records.poisoning_effects: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, dizziness, headache, confusion, delirium, seizures, coma, hepatitis, jaundice, clotting problems, and possible death.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Any brain-like, wrinkled, folded, saddle-shaped, or beefsteak-looking spring mushroom is a hard no unless it is confirmed as true Morchella by lengthwise cut and cap attachment.
records.field_identification: Wrinkled, folded, lobed, saddle-like, or brain-like cap rather than a regular pitted honeycomb cap. Often reddish brown, chestnut, mahogany, tan, or darker brown; color varies by species and age. Interior is not cleanly hollow like true morels; stem may be solid, chambered, cottony, or irregular inside. Cap attachment differs from true morels and can be irregular rather than neatly attached along the lower edge. Often appears in spring during or near morel season, sometimes close to true morel habitat. No gills; this is an ascomycete false-morel form, but it belongs in searchable mushroom danger records.
records.overview: Searchable danger entry for False Morels / Gyromitra Group. Included because it can be confused with edible mushrooms in Upper Michigan / northern Great Lakes foraging contexts.
records.look_alike_notes: Morels: True morels have a pitted cap and are hollow inside from top to bottom when cut lengthwise. Gyromitra is folded/brain-like and may be solid, chambered, or cottony inside. Half Free Morel: Half-free morels have a true morel pit pattern and a partially attached cap; false morels can have irregular folds and unsafe internal structure. Verpa Group: Verpa species are also non-edible look-alikes in the app; cap attachment and interior structure should be used to keep both Verpa and Gyromitra out of edible morel decisions.

underside: no gills; spore-bearing surface on folded/lobed cap
substrate: on ground in woods, forest edges, burns, sandy soils, or disturbed spring habitats depending species
mushroom_profile.season_note: spring, early summer in colder northern sites
underside_color: similar to cap or paler, species-dependent
mushroom_profile.spore_print: not applicable/useful in the usual cap-and-paper sense; microscopy may be needed for species-level Gyromitra identification

records.mushroom_card_note: If it is not hollow from cap tip through stem base and does not have true morel pits, do not eat it. Any brain-like, wrinkled, folded, or beefsteak-looking spring mushroom is a hard no.
records.edibility_detail: Poisonous; some cases can be severe or fatal. Not a safe substitute for true morels.
records.toxicity_notes: Gyromitrin/monomethylhydrazine-type toxin risk; can affect GI tract, liver, kidneys, nervous system, and blood.
records.risk_reason: Gyromitra species may contain gyromitrin, which converts to monomethylhydrazine-type toxins. Toxicity is variable; one person may get little effect while another becomes seriously ill.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract, liver, kidneys, nervous system, and blood; nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, dizziness, headache, confusion, delirium, seizures, coma, hepatitis, jaundice, and clotting problems can occur.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Wrinkled, lobed, brain-like, saddle-shaped, or irregular caps; cap may not be fully attached like a true morel; interior may be chambered rather than cleanly hollow.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat false morels, beefsteak morels, Verpa, or any morel-like mushroom unless it is confirmed as a true Morchella and cut lengthwise to verify a hollow interior and proper cap attachment.
records.caution_reason: Gyromitrin risk and true-morel confusion; “looks kind of like a morel” is not enough.

records.scientific_name: Amanita phalloides
records.poisoning_effects: Contains amatoxins; poisoning symptoms can be delayed and can progress to liver and kidney failure, coma, and death. Cooking, freezing, boiling, or drying does not make it safe.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Included only as a deadly Amanita caution record.
records.field_identification: Amanita with white free gills, white spore print, ring on the stalk, and sack-like volva/cup at the base. Cap color is variable and can be greenish, yellowish, brownish, tan, or rarely white, so color alone is not a safe ID feature.
records.overview: Deadly Amanita caution stub for records that mention death caps or deadly Amanitas as look-alikes.
records.look_alike_notes: The volva/cup at the base may be partly underground. Dig up the whole base for identification and do not rely on cap color or taste.

records.scientific_name: Small to medium Lepiota species capable of containing amatoxins, with Lepiota subincarnata / deadly parasol as an anchor example. Treat as a group unless a local specimen is expertly identified.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat small Lepiota/dapperling-type mushrooms.
records.overview: Searchable danger entry for Deadly Lepiota Group. Included because it can be confused with edible mushrooms in Upper Michigan / northern Great Lakes foraging contexts.
records.notes: Safety-group entry for small lepiotoid mushrooms because some Lepiota species contain amatoxins and can cause fatal poisoning.
records.general_notes: Safety-group entry for small lepiotoid mushrooms because some Lepiota species contain amatoxins and can cause fatal poisoning.
records.field_identification: Small to medium parasol-like mushrooms with free white gills. White spore print. Cap often has brown, pink-brown, orange-brown, or reddish-brown scales on a paler/white background. Stem may have bands, felty material, or a faint ring/ring zone rather than a strong movable ring. Can grow in lawns, mulch, gardens, and woods depending species. Species-level ID may require microscopy; do not use smell or attractive appearance as safety clues.

cap_surface: velvety material breaking into concentric scales
ring: faint ring/band or velvety veil remnants; variable
substrate: lawns, mulch, gardens, broadleaf/mixed woods, disturbed soil
smell: indistinct, sweet/fruity, sharp, or unpleasant depending species; do not rely on smell
flesh_color: may pinken where damaged in some species
staining: some species may stain pinkish; not a safe separator

records.look_alike_notes: Fairy Ring Mushroom: Fairy ring mushrooms have a smooth tan cap and tough smooth stem; deadly Lepiota has scaly cap material and often felty/scaly stem bands. records.scientific_name: Chlorophyllum molybdites, also called false parasol, green-spored lepiota, or 'the vomiter'.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Green spore print means stop.
records.overview: Searchable danger entry for Green-spored Parasol. Included because it can be confused with edible mushrooms in Upper Michigan / northern Great Lakes foraging contexts.
records.notes: Important poisonous lawn mushroom and parasol look-alike. The greenish spore print is the key field clue when mature specimens are available.
records.general_notes: Important poisonous lawn mushroom and parasol look-alike. The greenish spore print is the key field clue when mature specimens are available.
records.field_identification: Large parasol-like lawn mushroom with broad white to brown cap and tan/cinnamon/pinkish-brown scales. Usually forms complete or partial fairy rings in lawns or grassy areas. White young gills become greenish-gray as spores mature. Large persistent ring on the stem; stem white to brownish and may discolor slightly brown when handled. Dull grayish-green spore print is the critical field mark. Young specimens with white gills can be confused with edible parasol/shaggy-parasol types.

staining: flesh usually not staining; may stain reddish brown to pale pinkish red near stem base

records.mushroom_card_note: Do not eat any parasol-like lawn mushroom with greenish gills or a green/gray-green spore print. Young white-gilled parasols are not beginner food either.
records.look_alike_notes: Shaggy Parasol: Shaggy parasols have white spore prints, not dull greenish prints; young green-spored parasols may still have white gills, so mature spores matter. Parasol Mushroom: True parasols are not a safe shortcut in Upper Michigan app logic; green-spored parasol is close enough in shape that spore print and gill color must be checked. Meadow Mushroom Agaricus Group: Agaricus gills mature pink to chocolate brown with a dark brown spore print; green-spored parasol matures greenish-gray with a dull green spore print.
records.risk_reason: Common lawn parasol that can be mistaken for edible parasols.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract; severe nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and cramps, sometimes with blood in vomit or stool.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Large white parasol-like lawn mushroom with cap scales, free gills, ring on stalk, gills that turn gray-green, and green spore print.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat parasol-like lawn mushrooms unless spore print and species are confirmed. Green spore print means do not eat.
records.caution_reason: Green-spored parasol causes significant GI poisoning and looks edible to beginners.

records.overview: Safety note for plain white, cream, tan, or brown gilled mushrooms that may resemble edible gilled mushrooms to a casual picker. This entry is kept as a group because several similar species share muscarine-poisoning risk, but the individual species are not identical.
records.notes: Plain white, cream, tan, or brown gilled mushrooms are not safe honey-mushroom substitutes. Use this as a caution flag, then compare with specific records such as Fool's funnel and White fibrecap where available.
records.general_notes: Group-level warning for similar-looking poisonous funnel and fibercap mushrooms; individual species can differ in habitat, spore color, odor, and exact appearance.
records.field_identification: Treat small to medium plain white, cream, tan, or brown gilled mushrooms as unsafe unless identified to species. Clitocybe-type mushrooms often have pale spores and attached to decurrent gills; Inocybe-type mushrooms often have silky or fibrous caps, brown spores, and may show a small cobwebby veil when young. These do not confirm honey mushrooms: honey mushrooms should be clustered from wood or roots, often with tougher fibrous stems, white spores, and Armillaria-specific features.

records.scientific_name: Clitocybe rivulosa / Clitocybe dealbata complex
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat small pale funnel-shaped Clitocybe-like mushrooms.
records.overview: Small pale funnel-shaped gilled mushroom associated with lawns, grassy openings, and sometimes fairy rings. It is included because plain pale gilled mushrooms can be mistaken for edible species by casual pickers.
records.notes: Part of a broader muscarine-risk group of pale funnel mushrooms. Similar species may differ in exact cap tint, gill attachment, odor, and habitat; do not use this entry as permission to identify every pale funnel to species by sight alone.
records.general_notes: Avoid small pale funnel-shaped gilled mushrooms unless identified by an expert-level key. They are not honey mushrooms and should not be collected as edible gilled mushrooms.
records.field_identification: Small white to buff or pinkish-tan cap, often flattened to depressed or funnel-like; crowded pale gills attached to or running down the stem; white to cream spore print; usually on grass or open ground rather than fruiting in dense woody Armillaria clusters. Lacks the tough clustered wood/root growth, honey-colored cap scales, and Armillaria-style growth pattern expected from honey mushrooms.
records.commonness: Occasional; exact local frequency needs regional review

records.scientific_name: Inocybe geophylla complex
records.overview: Small white to cream fibrous-capped gilled mushroom associated with hardwoods and conifers. Included because small pale fibercaps can be mistaken for harmless edible gilled mushrooms by casual pickers.
records.notes: Part of the broader Inocybe/fibercap warning group. Similar fibercap species may differ in cap color, odor, spore shape, and microscopic traits, so this entry is a safety anchor rather than a shortcut for identifying all Inocybe species.
records.general_notes: Avoid small fibrous-capped white, cream, tan, or lilac Inocybe-type mushrooms as food. Species-level ID can be difficult and may require microscopy.
records.field_identification: Small conical to bell-shaped cap becoming convex, usually white to cream and silky or fibrous; thin stem without a ring; gills pale at first, then grayish to brown as spores mature; brown spore print. Grows from soil with trees, not in dense woody honey-mushroom clusters from stumps, roots, or buried wood.
records.commonness: Widespread; exact local frequency needs regional review

records.scientific_name: Hypholoma fasciculare
records.overview: Common clustered wood-growing gilled mushroom with yellow to greenish-yellow tones, purple-brown spores, and a bitter taste. Included as a poisonous/caution look-alike for honey mushrooms and other clustered wood mushrooms.
records.notes: Common clustered wood-growing gilled mushroom with yellow to greenish-yellow tones, purple-brown spores, and a bitter taste. Included as a poisonous/caution look-alike for honey mushrooms and other clustered wood mushrooms.
records.general_notes: Common clustered wood-growing gilled mushroom with yellow to greenish-yellow tones, purple-brown spores, and a bitter taste. Included as a poisonous/caution look-alike for honey mushrooms and other clustered wood mushrooms.
records.field_identification: Look for tight clusters on dead wood, stumps, or buried wood; yellow to greenish-yellow gills that become olive, then spotted purplish brown; thin yellow flesh; faint ring zone or disappearing cortina; purple-brown spore print. The bitter taste is a clue in expert work, but this guide does not recommend tasting unknown mushrooms.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Bitter yellow-green clustered mushrooms from wood are off the table.
records.look_alike_notes: Compared with honey mushrooms, sulfur tuft has yellow to greenish-yellow gills that darken olive/purple-brown, a purple-brown spore print, and no sturdy Armillaria-style ring. Compared with brick cap, sulfur tuft is usually more yellow-green and bitter; brick cap has a brick-red cap center and gray to purple-gray gills.

staining: Rusty brown staining from base upward
mushroom_profile.season_note: Fall and colder weather; sometimes spring.

records.scientific_name: Pholiota squarrosa
records.overview: Large scaly brown-spored mushroom growing in clusters on wood or at tree bases. Included mainly as a honey-mushroom look-alike and caution species.
records.notes: Large scaly brown-spored mushroom growing in clusters on wood or at tree bases. Included mainly as a honey-mushroom look-alike and caution species.
records.general_notes: Large scaly brown-spored mushroom growing in clusters on wood or at tree bases. Included mainly as a honey-mushroom look-alike and caution species.
records.field_identification: Look for conspicuous buff to tawny scales on both cap and stem, attached gills that become greenish-yellow then rusty brown, a ring or ring zone, cinnamon-brown spore print, and clustered growth on hardwood or conifer wood or at tree bases.
records.culinary_uses: Do not treat as a food mushroom in this guide. It can resemble honey mushrooms from a distance but has brown spores, attached gills that become rusty brown, and heavy shaggy scales on cap and stem.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; included for identification and honey-mushroom comparison.
records.look_alike_notes: Honey mushrooms have white spores and usually smoother caps with Armillaria-style rings/rhizomorphs; shaggy scalycap has a heavily scaly cap and stem, attached gills that age rusty brown, and a cinnamon-brown spore print. Sulfur tuft is smaller/yellower with purple-brown spores and greenish gills.

records.scientific_name: Hygrophoropsis aurantiaca
records.overview: Orange, soft-fleshed gilled mushroom that superficially resembles chanterelles but has true, repeatedly forked orange gills rather than blunt chanterelle ridges.
records.notes: Orange, soft-fleshed gilled mushroom that superficially resembles chanterelles but has true, repeatedly forked orange gills rather than blunt chanterelle ridges.
records.general_notes: Orange, soft-fleshed gilled mushroom that superficially resembles chanterelles but has true, repeatedly forked orange gills rather than blunt chanterelle ridges.
records.field_identification: Look for a soft, flimsy orange to brownish-orange cap, true blade-like gills that run down the stem and fork repeatedly, white spore print, and growth on forest litter or woody debris, often under conifers. True chanterelles are firmer and have blunt ridges, not thin detachable gills.
records.culinary_uses: Not recommended. This guide treats false chanterelle as a caution/look-alike, not as a food mushroom.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide because it is easily confused with chanterelles by beginners and has a history of gastrointestinal caution in field guides.
records.look_alike_notes: True chanterelles have blunt, shallow ridges and firm flesh; false chanterelle has true, thin, repeatedly forked orange gills and softer flesh. Jack-o-lanterns have true gills and grow from wood or buried wood in clusters; they are more robust and poisonous.

records.scientific_name: Cortinarius species with emphasis on orellanine-containing or suspected nephrotoxic webcaps, including the Michigan-reported Cortinarius orellanosus case and broader Cortinarius warning traits.
records.overview: Searchable danger entry for Cortinarius / Webcap Warning Group. Included because it can be confused with edible mushrooms in Upper Michigan / northern Great Lakes foraging contexts.
records.field_identification: Usually terrestrial/mycorrhizal in woods, not truly growing from wood. Young mushrooms often have a cobwebby partial veil called a cortina between cap margin and stem. Rusty brown spore print; mature gills are often stained rusty brown by spores. Colors vary wildly: brown, orange-brown, yellow-brown, purple, violet, red-gilled, slimy, dry, scaly, or plain. Some species resemble blewits or other edible gilled woodland mushrooms. Species-level identification is often difficult; many require microscopy and expert keys.
records.mushroom_card_note: Do not eat Cortinarius. Rusty-brown spores plus a cobwebby veil/cortina in a woodland mushroom means it is not food for this guide.
records.edibility_detail: Dangerous group; not edible for this app.
records.look_alike_notes: Wood Blewit: Wood blewits have pale pinkish spore prints, not rusty brown, and lack a rusty cortina. Purple color alone is not enough. Gymnopilus Group: Gymnopilus usually grows from wood and has orange to rusty spores; Cortinarius is usually ground-growing/mycorrhizal, but buried wood can confuse beginners. Tricholoma Group: Tricholoma typically has white spores and no cortina; Cortinarius has rusty-brown spores and cortina evidence.
records.poisoning_effects: Delayed nausea, vomiting, lethargy, thirst, frequent urination, headache, chills, and progressive kidney failure.
records.toxicity_notes: Some species contain orellanine with delayed kidney-failure risk.

stem_color: often similar to cap or paler
stem_feature: rusty spore deposits on cortina remnants/stem

smell: variable; not a safe edible/poisonous separator
substrate: on ground in woods; mycorrhizal with trees

records.scientific_name: Scleroderma species, with Scleroderma citrinum as the main common-earthball anchor; includes Scleroderma aurantium/vulgare synonym context where used by sources.
records.overview: Searchable danger entry for Earthballs / Pigskin Poison Puffballs. Included because it can be confused with edible mushrooms in Upper Michigan / northern Great Lakes foraging contexts.
records.field_identification: Round to flattened puffball-like fruiting body with no true cap, gills, pores, or stem. Outer skin/rind is tough, thick, yellowish to yellow-brown, and often coarse-scaly or cracked like pigskin. Cut open: interior quickly becomes purple-gray, purple-black, blackish brown, or mottled dark; not pure white throughout like an edible puffball. Mature interior becomes powdery dark spore mass. Often attached at base to whitish/yellowish rhizomorphs. Can smell rubbery, unpleasant, or gas-like in some descriptions.
records.mushroom_card_note: Cut every puffball-like mushroom open. If the inside is not pure white and uniform like cream cheese, do not eat it. Dark, purple, black, marbled, or tough pigskin interior means no.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Slice every puffball-like mushroom from top to bottom; any dark, veined, purple, brown, black, powdery, or tough interior means stop.
records.look_alike_notes: Puffballs: Edible puffballs must be pure white and uniform inside when sliced. Earthballs have tough skin and a darkening purple-black/blackish interior. Pear Shaped Puffball: Pear-shaped puffballs usually grow on rotting wood and are softer; earthballs are tough, pigskin-like, and dark inside. Truffles: Inexperienced collectors sometimes mistake earthballs for truffles; slicing reveals the dark spore mass and tough rind.
records.poisoning_effects: Abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, vertigo, altered consciousness, low blood pressure, slow or fast abnormal pulse in some reports.
records.toxicity_notes: Can cause gastrointestinal illness and sometimes neurologic/cardiovascular symptoms.

staining: fresh rind may blush yellowish or reddish when sliced
mushroom_profile.spore_print: not a normal cap spore print; mature gleba/spore mass dark purple-black to blackish brown
substrate: on ground in woods, paths, disturbed soil, around trees; mycorrhizal

records.risk_reason: Yellow-staining Agaricus can look like edible meadow/field Agaricus but causes gastrointestinal illness and is separated by bright yellow staining and phenolic/chemical odor.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract: nausea, stomach cramps, diarrhea, and vomiting, often soon after eating.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Bright yellow staining, especially at the stem base or cap edge; yellow flesh in the stem base when cut; phenolic, ink-like, iodine-like, chemical, or disinfectant odor; free gills that age pink to chocolate-brown.
records.hard_rule: Reject any Agaricus that stains bright yellow at the stem base or smells chemical/phenolic like ink, iodine, kerosene, or disinfectant.

records.scientific_name: Verpa bohemica, Verpa conica, and similar free-hanging Verpa species
records.safety: Not edible in this guide / morel look-alike caution
records.risk_reason: Verpa species are spring morel look-alikes with a cap attached only at the top and a stem that may be cottony or pithy inside. They are not treated as edible morels in this app.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Primarily gastrointestinal caution; some reports include neurologic-type symptoms. Treat as avoid for app safety.
records.field_identification: Free-hanging cap attached only at the top of the stem; cap hangs like a thimble or skirt; stem often has cottony pith or wisps rather than being cleanly hollow. Verpa bohemica has a wrinkled cap; Verpa conica is smoother.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Free-hanging cap attached only at the top of the stem; cap hangs like a thimble or skirt; stem often has cottony pith or wisps rather than being cleanly hollow. Verpa bohemica has a wrinkled cap; Verpa conica is smoother.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat free-hanging “early morels”: if the cap is attached only at the top or the stem has cottony pith instead of being cleanly hollow, treat it as Verpa and reject it.
records.caution_reason: Verpa species are spring morel look-alikes with a cap attached only at the top and a stem that may be cottony or pithy inside. They are not treated as edible morels in this app.
records.look_alike_notes: Do not eat free-hanging “early morels”: if the cap is attached only at the top or the stem has cottony pith instead of being cleanly hollow, treat it as Verpa and reject it.

records.display_name: Alcohol Inky Cap / Common Ink Cap
records.common_name: Alcohol Inky Cap / Common Ink Cap
common_names: Alcohol Inky Cap / Common Ink Cap
records.scientific_name: Coprinopsis atramentaria
records.safety: Conditionally poisonous with alcohol / not recommended in this guide
records.risk_reason: Coprine-containing inky caps can trigger a disulfiram-like reaction if alcohol is consumed before or after eating them, and they can be confused with true shaggy mane by beginners.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Cardiovascular/autonomic and gastrointestinal: flushing, headache, nausea, vomiting, rapid heartbeat, palpitations, tingling, and feeling ill when alcohol is involved.
records.field_identification: Smooth gray to gray-brown cap, often clustered; gills blacken and liquefy into ink; grows around stumps, buried wood, roots, lawns, or disturbed ground. Lacks the tall white shaggy cylinder of classic shaggy mane.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Smooth gray to gray-brown cap, often clustered; gills blacken and liquefy into ink; grows around stumps, buried wood, roots, lawns, or disturbed ground. Lacks the tall white shaggy cylinder of classic shaggy mane.
records.hard_rule: Do not treat smooth gray inky caps as shaggy manes; Alcohol Inky Cap can cause coprine poisoning if alcohol is consumed before or after the meal.

records.scientific_name: Button-stage white Amanitas, especially Amanita bisporigera / destroying angel group
records.risk_reason: Young Amanita buttons can resemble puffballs or edible white button mushrooms before their gills and base are obvious. Some contain amatoxins.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Liver and kidneys; delayed vomiting/diarrhea may be followed by apparent recovery, then liver injury, kidney failure, coma, and possible death.
records.field_identification: Young Amanita may be egg-like or button-shaped. When cut, it can show a hidden cap, stem, gills, ring, or basal cup/volva structure. Mature forms have white gills, white spore print, ring, and a cup/volva at the base that may be buried.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Young Amanita may be egg-like or button-shaped. When cut, it can show a hidden cap, stem, gills, ring, or basal cup/volva structure. Mature forms have white gills, white spore print, ring, and a cup/volva at the base that may be buried.
records.hard_rule: Slice every puffball-like mushroom top-to-bottom; if you see any hidden cap, stem, gills, ring, or cup/volva structure, treat it as a possible deadly Amanita button and do not eat it.

records.scientific_name: Poisonous or unknown Entoloma species with pink spores/gills
records.risk_reason: Many Entoloma/pinkgill species are difficult to identify and some are poisonous. Pink spores alone are not a food signal.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Primarily gastrointestinal for poisonous Entoloma-type cases: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramps, and sometimes headache; severity varies by species.
records.field_identification: Pink spore print; gills often attached rather than free; often grows from soil, leaf litter, or near wood rather than cleanly from wood; many species require microscopy. Gray gilled Entoloma-like mushrooms should not be eaten casually.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Pink spore print; gills often attached rather than free; often grows from soil, leaf litter, or near wood rather than cleanly from wood; many species require microscopy. Gray gilled Entoloma-like mushrooms should not be eaten casually.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat pink-spored gray gilled mushrooms unless the gills, substrate, and species are confirmed; poisonous Entoloma can resemble deer mushrooms or the gilled form near shrimp-of-the-woods.
records.caution_reason: Many Entoloma/pinkgill species are difficult to identify and some are poisonous. Pink spores alone are not a food signal.

records.scientific_name: Pleurocybella porrigens
records.risk_reason: Angel Wing is a thin white oyster-like mushroom of conifer wood. Because it can be confused with edible oysters and has a history of poisoning concern, this app treats it as a do-not-eat look-alike.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Poisoning reports are debated and not treated here as a simple edible; avoid for public field-guide safety.
records.field_identification: Thin pure-white oyster-like shelves, often with little or no stem, growing on conifer wood; more delicate and thinner than fleshy Pleurotus oysters.
